# CSS Guide for JTD-RMW

*This document was generated by Claude (2026-03-16) and may contain inaccuracies. Verify against the actual CSS before relying on it.*

This document describes how the stylesheet in `assets/css/compiled-jtd-style.css` works, what tricks it uses, and what CSS classes are available. The stylesheet is a hand-compiled artifact — not generated by Sass — so this guide serves as the authoritative reference for what's inside.

## File Structure

The main stylesheet imports three companion files:

```css
@import url("colorscheme.css");
@import url("extrabits.css");
/* ... main styles ... */
@import url("kineticgraphs.css");
```

- **`colorscheme.css`** defines color variables for light and dark mode.
- **`extrabits.css`** is an empty placeholder for consuming sites to inject custom CSS.
- **`kineticgraphs.css`** styles for the [kgjs](https://github.com/cmakler/kgjs) interactive graph library. Imported at the very end.

The main file itself is organized into labeled sections with ASCII art dividers:

1. Normalize.css (line ~50)
2. Basic Layout (line ~100)
3. Lists (line ~400)
4. Breadcrumbs (line ~450)
5. Headers (line ~500)
6. Search Widget (line ~600)
7. Navigation Sidebar (line ~1000)
8. Tables (line ~1100)
9. Printing (line ~1200)
10. Tweaks and Additions (line ~1300)

## Color Scheme

All colors are defined as CSS custom properties in `colorscheme.css`, with a dark mode override via `prefers-color-scheme`:

```css
:root {
    --basecolor: #fdf6e3;     /* page background (solarized light) */
    --boxcolor: #eee8d5;      /* sidebar, code blocks, table headers */
    --feedbackcolor: #e7dfc5; /* hover highlights */
    --accentcolor: #7a0019;   /* links, icons (UMN maroon) */
    --bordercolor: #93a1a1;   /* borders, horizontal rules */
    --textcolor: #55525B;     /* body text */
    --titlecolor: #27262b;    /* headings */
}

@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
    :root {
        --basecolor: #000;
        --boxcolor: #222;
        --feedbackcolor: #411;
        --accentcolor: #f99;
        --bordercolor: #93a1a1;
        --textcolor: #fee;
        --titlecolor: #fff;
    }
}
```

The light theme is solarized-inspired with UMN maroon as the accent. The dark theme inverts to black with a warm salmon accent. The `--bordercolor` is the same in both modes.

Every color in the main stylesheet references these variables, so swapping the color scheme is a matter of overriding seven custom properties. Consuming sites can do this in `extrabits.css` or their own `head_custom.html`.

## Typography

The stylesheet uses a dual-font strategy: sans-serif for UI and headings, serif for body text.

```css
body {
    font-family: Roboto, sans-serif;
}
main {
    font-family: Merriweather, 'Roboto Slab', serif;
}
main h1, main h2, main h3, main h4, main h5, main h6, main hgroup {
    font-family: Roboto, sans-serif;
}
```

Everything outside `<main>` (the sidebar, search, breadcrumbs, footer) renders in Roboto. The main content area switches to Merriweather for readability, but headings inside `<main>` revert to Roboto so they match the rest of the UI.

The base font size scales responsively:

```css
html { font-size: 14px !important; }
@media (min-width: 31.25rem) {
    html { font-size: 16px !important; }
}
```

14px on narrow screens, 16px on anything wider than 500px (31.25rem).

## Layout

### The sidebar/main split

The page is a two-column layout: a `.side-bar` on the left and `.main` on the right. On mobile, the sidebar collapses into a top bar with a hamburger menu.

```css
.side-bar {
    z-index: 0;
    display: flex;
    flex-wrap: wrap;
    background-color: var(--boxcolor);
}
@media (min-width: 80rem) {
    .side-bar {
        flex-wrap: nowrap;
        position: fixed;
        width: 248px;
        height: 100%;
        flex-direction: column;
        border-right: 1px solid var(--bordercolor);
    }
}
@media (min-width: 80rem) {
    .main { margin-left: 248px; }
}
```

At 80rem (1280px) and above, the sidebar becomes a fixed 248px column and the main content shifts right to compensate. Below that breakpoint, the sidebar is a flex row that wraps — the site header and hamburger button sit side by side, and the nav is hidden until toggled.

### Content width cap

```css
@media (min-width: 50rem) {
    main > *:not(.table-wrapper-wide):not(.full-width) {
        max-width: 800px;
    }
}
```

On screens wider than 800px, all direct children of `<main>` are capped at 800px — except elements with the `.table-wrapper-wide` or `.full-width` classes, which are allowed to span the full content area. This keeps body text at a comfortable reading width while allowing tables and other wide content to breathe.

## CSS Tricks

### Flipped `<details>` marker for navigation

The sidebar navigation uses `<details>`/`<summary>` elements for collapsible groups. By default, the disclosure triangle sits on the left. The stylesheet flips it to the right using a double `scaleX(-1)` trick:

```css
.site-nav summary {
    transform: scaleX(-1);          /* flip the whole summary, moving the marker to the right */
}
.site-nav summary > .nav-list-link {
    transform: scaleX(-1);          /* flip the link text back so it reads normally */
    position: absolute;
    right: 0.5em;
    width: calc(100% - 2rem);
    background-color: var(--boxcolor);
}
```

The `<summary>` is horizontally mirrored, which puts the browser's native disclosure triangle on the right side. Then the link text inside is mirrored again to restore normal reading direction. The link is absolutely positioned and given a background so it covers the mirrored layout cleanly.

### Tufte-style sidenotes with `<aside>`

The stylesheet implements Tufte-inspired margin notes that float into the right margin on wide screens:

```css
aside {
    font-size: .9rem;
    font-style: italic;
    padding: 1rem;
    background-color: var(--basecolor);
    border-radius: 1.5rem;
    border-left: 3px solid var(--bordercolor);
    border-right: 3px solid var(--bordercolor);
}
@media (min-width: 64rem) {
    aside, .aside-like {
        float: right;
        clear: right;
        margin-right: max(-2rem, 100% - 800px - 28rem);
        width: min(20rem, 100% - 800px + 1rem);
        margin-top: -6rem;
        margin-bottom: 6rem;
    }
}
```

On screens narrower than 64rem, `<aside>` elements render inline as bordered blocks. On wider screens, they float right and use `max()` and `min()` to position themselves in the margin gap between the 800px content column and the viewport edge. The `margin-top: -6rem` pulls them upward so they sit alongside the paragraph that precedes them, rather than below it. The `.aside-like` class provides the same positioning for elements that aren't semantically asides.

### Gradient hover effects

Links in the navigation and the site title use a directional gradient instead of a flat background color for hover feedback:

```css
.nav-list .nav-list-item .nav-list-link:hover,
.nav-list .nav-list-item .nav-list-link.active {
    background-image: linear-gradient(-90deg, var(--feedbackcolor) 0%, var(--feedbackcolor) 70%, #0000 100%);
}
```

The gradient goes from the feedback color at 0–70% to fully transparent at 100%, fading out on the left edge. This gives the hover a soft trailing edge rather than a hard box, which looks less heavy in a navigation list.

### Table wrapper negative margins

The `.table-wrapper` breaks out of the content padding to give tables more room:

```css
.table-wrapper {
    margin-right: -1rem;
    margin-left: -1rem;
    padding: 1rem;
    overflow-x: auto;
    box-shadow: 0 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.12), 0 3px 10px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.08);
}
```

The negative margins pull the wrapper flush with the edges of `.main-content-wrap`, effectively reclaiming the content area's padding. The padding inside the wrapper then restores the spacing for the table itself. The result is a table that appears to span the full width of the content area, with a subtle box shadow to visually separate it. The `.table-wrapper-wide` variant goes even further, using `calc(100% + 4rem)` to span the full viewport width within the main area.

### Selection color

```css
::selection {
    color: var(--basecolor);
    background: var(--accentcolor);
}
```

Text selection is styled in the accent color (maroon in light mode, salmon in dark mode) with the base color as the text, creating a high-contrast inversion effect.

### Link styling with class exclusion

```css
a { color: var(--accentcolor); text-decoration: none; }
a:not([class]) { text-decoration: underline; }
a:not([class]):hover { background-color: var(--feedbackcolor); }
```

All links get the accent color. But only links *without* a CSS class get underlines and hover highlights. This means navigation links, the site title, and other UI elements with classes (`.nav-list-link`, `.site-title`, etc.) stay clean, while inline content links in body text get the expected underline. It's a simple way to differentiate structural links from content links without adding a class to every body link.

### Print stylesheet

The print styles hide all navigation chrome and make the page print cleanly:

```css
@media print {
    .site-footer, .site-header, .site-button,
    #edit-this-page, #back-to-top, .site-nav, .main-header {
        display: none !important;
    }
    .side-bar { width: 100%; height: auto; border-right: 0 !important; }
    .main { max-width: none; margin-left: 0; }
    body { background-color: #0000; color: black; }
    pre, code, .highlighter-rouge { background-color: #0000; }
}
```

The sidebar, footer, search bar, and edit links are all hidden. The main content expands to full width. Backgrounds on code blocks are removed so they don't waste ink. There's a commented-out `column-count: 2` that would enable two-column printing but is currently disabled.

## Class Index

### Theme structural classes (used by layouts/includes)

These are used by the HTML in `default.html`, `nav_details.html`, and other theme templates. You generally don't need to use them in page content.

| Class | Used by | Purpose |
|-------|---------|---------|
| `.side-bar` | `default.html` | The sidebar container |
| `.site-header` | `default.html` | The sidebar header (site title area) |
| `.site-title` | `default.html` | The site title link |
| `.site-button` | `default.html` | The hamburger menu button |
| `.site-nav` | `default.html` | The navigation container |
| `.site-footer` | `default.html` | The sidebar footer |
| `.sidebar-header` | `default.html` | Custom header area above nav |
| `.main` | `default.html` | The main content area |
| `.main-header` | `default.html` | The top bar (contains search on desktop) |
| `.main-content-wrap` | `default.html` | Padding wrapper around content |
| `.main-content` | `default.html` | The `<main>` element |
| `.nav-open` | `just-the-docs.js` | Toggled by JS to show/hide mobile nav |
| `.nav-list` | `nav_details.html` | Navigation list container |
| `.nav-list-item` | `nav_details.html` | Individual nav entry |
| `.nav-list-link` | `nav_details.html` | Navigation link |
| `.active` | `nav_details.html` | Applied to the current page's nav link |
| `.breadcrumb-nav` | `default.html` | Breadcrumb container |
| `.breadcrumb-nav-list` | `default.html` | Breadcrumb `<ol>` |
| `.breadcrumb-nav-list-item` | `default.html` | Individual breadcrumb |
| `.icon` | `default.html` | SVG icon sizing |
| `.anchor-heading` | `anchor_headings.html` | The `¶` heading anchor links |
| `.search-*` | `default.html` / JS | The search widget family (input, results, overlays, etc.) |
| `.date-header` | `post.html` | The right-aligned date line in the post layout |
| `.table-wrapper` | `table_wrappers.html` | Default responsive table wrapper |
| `.table-wrapper-wide` | `table_wrappers.html` | Full-width table wrapper |
| `.highlighter-rouge` | Jekyll/kramdown | Code block container (styled by the theme) |
| `.highlight` | Jekyll/kramdown | Syntax-highlighted code |
| `.text-small` | `default.html` footer | Small text in the footer area |

### Helper classes for page content

These are available for use in individual pages to control layout and behavior.

| Class | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `.full-width` | Exempts an element from the 800px `max-width` cap, allowing it to span the full content area. |
| `.aside-like` | Gives any element the same float-to-margin behavior as `<aside>` on wide screens, without requiring the `<aside>` tag. |
| `.pagebreak` | Forces a page break when printing. The element itself collapses to zero height on screen. |
| `.task-list` | Styled by the theme for GitHub-flavored markdown task lists (`- [ ]` / `- [x]`). Generated automatically by kramdown. |
| `.gameMatrix` | (In `kineticgraphs.css`) Styles a game theory payoff matrix table inside a kgjs container. |

### Semantic HTML elements with special styling

Several bare HTML elements receive significant custom styling without needing a class:

| Element | Styling |
|---------|---------|
| `<aside>` | Tufte-style margin note: italic, bordered, floats to the right margin on wide screens. |
| `<details>` / `<summary>` | Styled as bordered, rounded disclosure widgets with a colored summary bar. |
| `<blockquote>` | Left border, with `<cite>` children right-aligned and smaller. |
| `<figure>` / `<figcaption>` | Figure gets a background color; caption is centered, small, and italic. |
| `<hgroup>` with `<p>` | A `<p>` inside `<hgroup>` after an `<h1>` is styled as a centered subtitle (used by the `post` layout). |
| `<hr>` | Rendered as a 1px line in the border color. The element immediately following an `<hr>` has its top margin removed for tighter spacing. |
| `<dt>` / `<dd>` | Definition terms are bold with an auto-appended colon. Definition descriptions are indented and slightly smaller. |
| `.footnotes` | The kramdown footnotes container gets a top border and smaller font size. |

## Breakpoints

The stylesheet uses three main breakpoints:

| Breakpoint | Purpose |
|------------|---------|
| `31.25rem` (500px) | Font size bumps from 14px to 16px. Various elements get slightly larger text. |
| `50rem` (800px) | The 800px `max-width` cap kicks in on `<main>` children. Table wrappers get wider margins. |
| `64rem` (1024px) | `<aside>` elements float into the right margin. |
| `80rem` (1280px) | The sidebar becomes a fixed column. The main content shifts right. Search moves to the top bar. Mobile hamburger menu is hidden. |

## Notes

There's a commented-out margin trick in the "Tweaks" section that's worth knowing about:

```css
/*A note for future reference:
The following snippet can make an element in main cling to the side of the window.
margin-right: min(-1rem, -1 * (100vw - (100% + 20rem)) );
*/
```

This would push an element's right edge flush with the viewport, breaking out of the content column. It's the same kind of `min()`/`max()` technique used by the aside positioning.

The sidebar custom header and footer (`.sidebar-header`, `.site-footer`) are hidden on mobile via `@media (max-width: 80rem)`. This means content injected via `nav_header_custom.html` and `nav_footer_custom.html` is desktop-only.
